positioning: Letta, formerly MemGPT, is an open-source Python server and API platform for building stateful LLM agents with long-term memory, tool execution, model/provider routing, and production-oriented observability.

snapshot.What it is: A full agent runtime and service layer, not just a memory library. The repo contains a FastAPI server, CLI entrypoint, agent loops, memory models, tool execution, MCP integration, provider adapters, persistence models, migrations, tests, Docker assets, OpenAPI/Fern API artifacts, and observability wiring.
snapshot.Who it is for: Teams building stateful AI agents, memory-backed assistants, tool-using agents, multi-agent workflows, or an internal agent API that needs persistence, streaming, SDKs, and provider flexibility.
snapshot.Why it matters: It packages the hard parts around long-lived agents: memory blocks, archival/recall state, tools, sandboxing, provider abstraction, API surface, migrations, and traces. That makes it a useful reference architecture even when not adopted wholesale.
snapshot.When not to use it: Avoid using it as a tiny embedded helper library or a low-complexity chatbot scaffold. The service-oriented architecture, database/migration surface, and tool execution model are more than many prototypes need.

facts.Repository identity: README.md names the project 'Letta (formerly MemGPT)' and describes it as a way to build AI with advanced memory that can learn and self-improve over time.
facts.Primary language: Python 3.11+ package with pyproject.toml, uv.lock, 536 Python source files under letta/, and 166 Python test files under tests/.
facts.Runtime shape: Typer CLI entrypoint launches a server by default via letta.main:app; FastAPI is used for the REST API; Docker and compose files support service deployment with Postgres/pgvector.
facts.API surface: fern/openapi.json identifies 'Letta API' version 1.0.0 with 239 paths. Major tags include agents, tools, sources/folders, conversations, runs, blocks, MCP servers, providers, jobs, messages, and archives.
facts.Persistence: SQLAlchemy/SQLModel models live in letta/orm; alembic/versions contains 167 migrations; compose.yaml uses ankane/pgvector for PostgreSQL vector storage.
facts.Memory model: letta/schemas/memory.py defines core memory blocks, file blocks, context window overview, and git-enabled memory rendering paths.
facts.Tooling: Tool execution uses a factory over core, memory, file, builtin, MCP, multi-agent, and sandboxed executors; local, E2B, and Modal sandbox paths appear in services/tool_sandbox.
facts.Provider support: pyproject.toml and letta/llm_api include adapters for OpenAI, Anthropic, Azure, Bedrock, Google Gemini/Vertex, Groq, Mistral, DeepSeek, Ollama, OpenRouter, Together, vLLM, xAI, Z.ai, and others.
facts.Quality signals: The repository includes unit/integration/docker/migration workflows, ruff/ty config, pytest config, a security policy, AI contribution policy, Apache license, and a large tests directory.
facts.External dynamic metadata: GitHub stars/forks/open issues were not recorded because GitHub API returned rate-limit 403 during this run.

design_philosophy.claim: Stateful agents are modeled as service-owned entities rather than transient prompt calls.
design_philosophy.detail: Agents, messages, blocks, tools, sources, archives, runs, steps, and provider traces are all first-class resources with managers, ORM models, schemas, and API routes.

design_philosophy.claim: Memory is treated as editable structured state, not only vector retrieval.
design_philosophy.detail: Core memory blocks, archival/recall counts, file blocks, and git-enabled memory rendering show a layered memory design with both in-context and external projections.

design_philosophy.claim: The runtime favors pluggability across models, tools, storage, and execution backends.
design_philosophy.detail: Provider adapters are separated from the agent loop; tool executors are selected by ToolType; sandbox backends include local, E2B, and Modal; MCP has dedicated managers and clients.

design_philosophy.claim: The product surface is API-first, with SDK and documentation generation as part of the system.
design_philosophy.detail: OpenAPI generation appears in the FastAPI app, and Fern files/workflows are present for docs and Python/TypeScript SDK publishing.

design_philosophy.claim: Operational visibility is a core concern rather than an afterthought.
design_philosophy.detail: The app initializes Sentry/Datadog/OpenTelemetry paths, exposes run/step/trace endpoints, includes ClickHouse trace backends, and ships otel collector configs.

architecture.summary: Letta is best read as a layered agent platform: API/SDK clients call FastAPI routers, routers delegate to a SyncServer facade, the facade coordinates domain managers, managers persist through ORM/storage, and agent loops orchestrate prompts, memory, model calls, tools, streaming, runs, and traces.
architecture.mermaid:
flowchart TD
  User[CLI / Python SDK / TS SDK / REST clients] --> API[FastAPI REST API\nletta/server/rest_api]
  User --> CLI[Typer CLI\nletta/main.py]
  CLI --> API
  API --> Server[SyncServer facade\nletta/server/server.py]
  Server --> Managers[Domain managers\nAgent / Message / Block / Tool / Source / Run]
  Managers --> ORM[SQLAlchemy ORM + Alembic\nletta/orm + alembic]
  ORM --> DB[(Postgres / pgvector\nSQLite in tests/dev)]
  Managers --> Memory[Memory system\nblocks / archival / recall / git memory repo]
  Memory --> MemRepo[Object-backed Git memory\nservices/memory_repo]
  Server --> AgentLoop[AgentLoop factory\nV2 / V3 / sleeptime / voice]
  AgentLoop --> Prompt[Prompt + context assembly\nPromptGenerator / Memory.compile]
  AgentLoop --> LLM[LLMClient + provider adapters\nOpenAI / Anthropic / Gemini / Ollama / etc.]
  AgentLoop --> Tools[ToolExecutionManager]
  Tools --> Builtin[Core / memory / files tools]
  Tools --> MCP[MCP tools + servers]
  Tools --> Sandbox[Sandbox execution\nLocal / E2B / Modal]
  AgentLoop --> Streaming[Streaming responses + runs + steps]
  Server --> Observability[OTel / Datadog / Sentry / provider traces]
  API --> OpenAPI[Fern OpenAPI + SDK generation]

modules.name: CLI and entrypoint
modules.responsibility: Expose the letta command and default server launch path.
modules.notes: pyproject declares project script letta = letta.main:app.

modules.name: REST API
modules.responsibility: FastAPI app, middleware, exception handling, routers, OpenAPI generation, streaming endpoints.
modules.notes: OpenAPI surface is large: 239 paths in fern/openapi.json.

modules.name: Server facade
modules.responsibility: Owns global managers and provider setup; coordinates the app's domain services.
modules.notes: SyncServer initializes managers for agents, tools, memory, MCP, providers, runs, telemetry, files, jobs, groups, and more.

modules.name: Agent runtime
modules.responsibility: Load the correct agent loop and run blocking/streaming steps with messages, tool calls, credit checks, context, and stop reasons.
modules.notes: AgentLoop dispatches between V2, V3, sleeptime multi-agent, and voice variants.

modules.name: Memory system
modules.responsibility: Represent in-context memory, external memory summaries, blocks, git-backed memory projections, and file blocks.
modules.notes: Git-enabled memory treats git as source of truth and Postgres as cache for enabled agents.

modules.name: Persistence layer
modules.responsibility: Define database models, migrations, registry, and storage-backed state.
modules.notes: 167 Alembic versions indicate an actively evolved schema.

modules.name: Tool and sandbox system
modules.responsibility: Create, list, run, and sandbox tools; support built-in tools, MCP, files, memory, multi-agent, local/E2B/Modal backends.
modules.notes: Factory-based executor selection makes tool behavior extensible but also creates a broad security surface.

modules.name: Provider abstraction
modules.responsibility: Route model calls across many LLM and embedding providers.
modules.notes: Provider breadth is high; operational correctness depends on many external API behaviors.

modules.name: API artifact and SDK generation
modules.responsibility: Maintain OpenAPI and Fern-driven docs/SDK publishing workflows.
modules.notes: A strong signal that hosted/API usage is a first-class product path.

highlights.detail: The repo covers memory, agent runtime, API, SDK artifacts, provider adapters, tools, sandboxing, persistence, migrations, and observability in one coherent service.

highlights.claim: Memory architecture is more explicit than typical chat-agent projects
highlights.detail: Memory is modeled with blocks, archival/recall counts, file blocks, core memory rendering, and optional git-backed state history.
The codebase supports many LLM providers, MCP tools/servers, sandbox backends, Docker deployment, OpenAPI, Python and TypeScript client usage, and provider traces.
The repository has 166 Python test files, CI workflows for unit/integration/docker/migration paths, ruff/ty/pytest config, security policy, AI contribution policy, and Apache license.
The README leads with Letta API and SDK examples; FastAPI and Fern artifacts indicate the repo is meant to be operated as a service and consumed from applications.

highlights.claim: Useful reference for building production agent infrastructure
highlights.detail: Even teams that do not adopt Letta can study its separation of routers, managers, schemas, ORM, provider adapters, tool executors, sandboxing, and traces.

risks.detail: Letta is a service platform with many moving pieces: Postgres/pgvector, migrations, provider credentials, tool sandboxing, background scheduling, API auth/middleware, OpenAPI/SDK artifacts, and observability. This is powerful but not lightweight.

risks.claim: Security surface is broad because agents can execute tools
risks.detail: Tool execution supports local subprocesses, virtualenv setup, fetched credentials, environment variable injection, MCP, E2B, and Modal. Teams adopting it need strict sandbox and credential policies.

risks.claim: Fast-moving schema and API surface may create upgrade work
risks.detail: The repo has 167 Alembic migrations, many API routes, deprecated endpoints in sources/groups/identities, and active SDK/Fern workflows. Integrators should expect migration and compatibility chores.

risks.claim: README is product-focused and omits many operational details
risks.detail: README gives a concise API/CLI quickstart, but most operational complexity is spread across docs links, compose files, settings, workflows, migrations, and server code.

risks.claim: Provider breadth increases testing and runtime variability
risks.detail: Supporting many providers is valuable, but behavior differences across model APIs, streaming, tool calling, reasoning, caching, usage accounting, and rate limits will complicate reliability.

risks.claim: External repository popularity metrics are missing from this run
risks.detail: GitHub API returned rate-limit 403 during analysis, so stars/forks/open issues were not captured in the memory file. Refresh this data before investor, adoption, or trend comparisons.

scenarios.scenario: Build a stateful AI assistant with long-term memory
scenarios.why: This is the core positioning of Letta/MemGPT, and the codebase has explicit agent, memory, block, message, archive, and source abstractions.
scenarios.notes: Start with hosted Letta API or Docker deployment, then test memory block updates and retrieval behavior against your domain.

scenarios.scenario: Internal agent platform with REST API and SDK consumers
scenarios.why: FastAPI, OpenAPI/Fern, Python/TypeScript SDK flows, and many REST resources are already part of the repository shape.
scenarios.notes: Good fit if you want centralized agents, auth, runs, observability, and tool governance.

scenarios.why: Tool creation, MCP tools, local/E2B/Modal sandbox backends, and executor dispatch are built into the architecture.
scenarios.notes: Audit sandbox settings and credentials before production usage.

scenarios.why: The repo is a rich reference for memory blocks, agent loops, summarization, context window management, tool calls, and provider abstraction.
scenarios.notes: Useful even without direct adoption; study managers and schemas first.

scenarios.scenario: Small single-file chatbot or embedded library
scenarios.why: The repository is a service-oriented platform with persistence, migrations, routers, managers, and deployment concerns.
scenarios.notes: Use a smaller library or direct SDK approach unless memory/platform needs justify the overhead.

scenarios.why: Letta has observability, tests, security policy, and structured APIs, but tool execution and many provider integrations create a non-trivial risk/compliance surface.
scenarios.notes: Run a security review, disable unneeded sandboxes/providers, define credential isolation, and pin deployment versions.

peers.positioning: Stateful agent platform with long-term memory, tool execution, and API/SDK surface.
peers.strengths: Explicit memory model, broad provider/tool support, service architecture, strong API surface, tests and CI.
peers.weaknesses: Complex adoption surface; sandbox/provider/security governance required.
peers.best_fit: Teams building persistent AI agents or studying memory-agent infrastructure.

peers.positioning: Graph/state-machine framework for controllable agent workflows.
peers.strengths: Excellent for workflow graphs, control flow, checkpoints, and composable orchestration.
peers.weaknesses: Memory product/API platform concerns are less turnkey than Letta's service model.
peers.best_fit: Teams wanting custom agent orchestration with explicit graph control.

peers.strengths: Strong multi-agent experimentation model and conversation patterns.
peers.weaknesses: Long-term memory/platform API is not the central abstraction in the same way.

peers.strengths: Quick to express role-based workflows and task delegation.
peers.weaknesses: Less obviously a full memory server/API platform.
peers.best_fit: Workflow-style agent teams and business process prototypes.

peers.positioning: Hosted/model-provider agent tooling with managed model integration.
peers.strengths: Lower infrastructure burden and tight provider ecosystem integration.
peers.weaknesses: Less open-source control over persistence, memory internals, and provider-neutral runtime.
peers.best_fit: Teams preferring managed infrastructure and provider-native workflows.

adoption.Setup complexity: Medium to high. The quickstart can use hosted API/SDK paths, but self-hosting involves Docker or Python environment setup, Postgres/pgvector, migrations, provider credentials, and sandbox settings.
adoption.Integration path: For a product team, start from the Letta API and SDK examples in README.md. For a platform team, run Docker compose locally, inspect API resources with fern/openapi.json, then pilot one agent with memory blocks and a small controlled tool set.
adoption.Extension points: Custom tools, MCP servers, provider configuration, sandbox config, memory blocks, sources/folders, archives, prompts/templates, and provider trace backends.
adoption.Learning curve: High if modifying internals; medium if using the API. The manager/schema/ORM layering is consistent, but the domain is broad.
adoption.Suggested proof of concept: Create one agent with persona/human memory blocks, send messages, mutate memory, attach a source/folder, run one safe tool, inspect run/step traces, and test persistence across server restart.
